critic_raw:
Overall this is a carefully constructed experiment with clear scope limitations, good label hygiene, and honest acknowledgment of what it does and does not establish. That said, I see two genuine methodological issues worth flagging, and one borderline concern.

**Issue 1: Random stratified split on temporal data — the core validity threat**

The methodology stratifies and randomly shuffles 24 months of data into train/validation/test. This means transactions from, say, October 2023 can appear in training while transactions from February 2022 appear in test. This violates a fundamental assumption for time-series fraud data: card fraud is serially correlated at the customer, BIN, and merchant levels. A fraudster who hits 10 cards in a campaign will have some of those transactions in training and some in test — not because of feature leakage per se, but because the model sees the behavioral signature of that exact fraud cluster during training. The sequential features (7-day windows of prior transactions) compound this: the sequential context window for a test transaction can overlap temporally with training transactions from the same cardholder. Even if the feature is computed at authorization time, the pattern it captures was seen in training.

The practical consequence is that both models — but especially the sequential model — will appear to discriminate better than they will in production deployment, where future fraud campaigns are genuinely novel. The sequential model is especially vulnerable because its added value is precisely in capturing behavioral deviations from prior windows; if similar behavioral deviations were seen in training from the same customer or fraud cluster, the model learns the pattern from partially-seen sequences, inflating recall on held-out transactions that are temporally adjacent to training examples.

Evidence one way or the other: Compare recall improvement of the sequential features under (a) the stratified random split described, versus (b) a strict temporal split where training is Jan 2022–Jun 2023, validation is Jul–Sep 2023, and test is Oct–Dec 2023. If the sequential feature gain shrinks substantially under the temporal split, the random split is inflating results via temporal leakage of fraud campaigns. If the gain is stable, the concern is empirically bounded.

**Issue 2: Threshold selection and the 'acceptance check' create a soft data-dependent threshold**

The methodology states that after running the acceptance check on the test set, if the operating threshold appears 'materially misaligned,' the threshold will be refreshed using train+validation combined before the definitive evaluation pass. The evaluation is then reported on the test set. This is a conditional threshold update triggered by test-set behavior. Even if the model family is held fixed, the threshold is now adapted to the test set distribution — which is precisely the data being used for final reporting. The FPR budget check is not independent of the test partition; the protocol uses test-set FPR to decide whether to re-calibrate. This is a mild but real form of test-set peeking: the reported FPR will mechanically satisfy the 0.5% budget because the threshold was selected (or confirmed) with that outcome in mind. The recall improvement will also be slightly optimistic because the threshold is now implicitly fitted to the test marginal distribution rather than a truly held-out one.

Evidence one way or the other: Fix the threshold from validation only, never update it based on test-set feedback, and report whatever FPR falls out on the test set. Compare reported recall at that fixed threshold versus the recall reported after the adaptive refresh. If they differ materially, the adaptive protocol is injecting information from the test set into the reported operating point.

**Borderline concern: 120-day no-activity rule labels as legitimate**

This is a known and deliberate choice, but it quietly affects the sequential model more than the baseline. Transactions that are eventually disputed but fall outside the settlement window are labeled legitimate. These ambiguous cases are not randomly distributed — they are more common among low-engagement customers who may have irregular transaction histories, the exact population where sequential features (which depend on dense prior-window activity) have lower coverage. The result is that the sequential model's worst-case population (sparse prior windows) is disproportionately contaminated with ambiguous negatives, which could either inflate or deflate sequential model recall depending on whether those unresolved cases are true fraud or true legitimate. This is not a fatal flaw but it creates an asymmetric noise floor that is not discussed. Evidence: Compare model performance conditioned on prior-window transaction density to check whether the sequential gain holds across customers with sparse histories.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on temporal data allows serial fraud-campaign overlap between train and test, inflating sequential feature gains relative to their production value.

The conditional threshold refresh triggered by an 'acceptance check' on the test partition is a soft form of test-set peeking that mechanically satisfies the FPR budget and slightly optimizes reported recall.

The 120-day no-activity legitimate label creates an asymmetric ambiguous-negative floor concentrated in the sparse-history population where sequential features have lowest coverage, confounding sequential vs. tabular model comparison in that segment.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random stratified split on temporal data allows serial fraud-campaign overlap between train and test, inflating sequential feature gains relative to their production value.

The conditional threshold refresh triggered by an 'acceptance check' on the test partition is a soft form of test-set peeking that mechanically satisfies the FPR budget and slightly optimizes reported recall.

The 120-day no-activity legitimate label creates an asymmetric ambiguous-negative floor concentrated in the sparse-history population where sequential features have lowest coverage, confounding sequential vs. tabular model comparison in that segment.
